The second Trump administration has “really been a nail in the coffin when it comes to how American allies worldwide view American power,” added Hellyer.

title: From Omaha Steaks to hazmat suits: What life was like for contagion patients in quarantine
summary: As more than 40 Americans remain in quarantine for up to six weeks following a hantavirus outbreak aboard a cruise ship, former patients who spent time inside some of the country’s highest-security medical isolation units during previous viral contagions are sharing what it’s like to endure weeks cut off from the outside world.
story: As more than 40 Americans remain in quarantine for up to six weeks following a hantavirus outbreak aboard a cruise ship, former patients who spent time inside some of the country’s highest-security medical isolation units during previous viral contagions are sharing what it’s like to endure weeks cut off from the outside world.
Subscribe to read this story ad-free
Get unlimited access to ad-free articles and exclusive content.

“I want the people who are being affected by this, who are in quarantine or who have loved ones who are in quarantine, to rest assured that they are in the best of hands,” Dr. Kent Brantly, who spent weeks in isolation after contracting Ebola in 2014, told NBC News. “They are in the best place to be taken care of.”

The outbreak has killed three people and sickened 10 since it was first identified aboard the expedition cruise ship MV Hondius during its monthlong voyage in early May. The 18 Americans aboard the ship arrived stateside on Monday after days inside their cabins, before returning to quarantine in facilities designed to house people exposed to infectious diseases.

Two patients were being monitored at Emory University Hospital in Atlanta before joining the 16 others on Friday at the National Quarantine Unit at the University of Nebraska Medical Center in Omaha. None of the Americans has tested positive for the virus, though they could remain isolated for up to 42 days, the World Health Organization said Friday.

Brantly is among the few Americans who can relate to the cruise passengers’ ordeal. After contracting Ebola during a mission trip to Liberia in 2014, he spent three weeks inside Emory University Hospital’s biocontainment unit. The 11-bed unit, opened in 2005, has a dedicated laboratory and HEPA-filtered, negative-pressure rooms that prevent pathogens from escaping into all patient rooms.

As the first American Ebola patient to be treated at the facility, Brantly lived in a “decent-sized hospital room with its own bathroom” outfitted for intensive infection control. He was monitored around the clock, and a nurse wearing personal protective equipment was constantly at his bedside.

He was hooked up to monitors tracking his vital signs and administered IV fluids, underwent frequent blood draws and received an experimental treatment that had previously only been tested on animals.

“The team of doctors that attended to me were consummate professionals and experts in their field, but also just really incredible people,” Brantly said. “I have such confidence that the team there and at Nebraska are completely prepared to take care of a situation just like this one.”

Once he began regaining strength, Brantly said, nurses coached him through physical therapy exercises from inside the room. To pass the time, they played Nerf basketball together and spent hours talking about faith, family and life outside the hospital walls.
“They not only treated my medical condition, but they really cared for me holistically as a person,” Brantly said. “My physical well-being, but also my emotional and mental well-being.”
More than a decade later, hantavirus cruise passengers are experiencing some of the same rhythms of confinement Brantly remembers from his time inside Emory’s isolation unit.

Among them is Jake Rosmarin, a Boston-based social media creator who boarded the Hondius for a “content work trip” and has been inside Nebraska’s National Quarantine Unit since Monday. The only federally funded quarantine unit in the country features 20 single-occupancy rooms, each with a special air pressure system that filters clean air into each room. The building’s biocontainment unit, across the street, can fit up to 10 patients in five rooms. Currently, one of those rooms is being used as an in-house lab.

Since entering the unit, Rosmarin has been providing an inside look at life in quarantine via Instagram, with room tours, day-in-the-life videos and documentation of his first sip of iced coffee in weeks.

His room inside the Omaha facility includes a bed, a smart TV and a spin bike. Nurses deliver three meals a day, but he said he is allowed to order takeout — including Chipotle, which he was looking forward to ordering when he spoke to NBC News earlier this week.

Since his arrival, Rosmarin has decorated one wall with posters of major cities, added a blanket and a stuffed animal to his bed, and set up a tea station on one counter. From family care packages, Rosmarin said he has been enjoying puzzles and a charcuterie board he had to break down into zip-close bags because it was too big to finish in one sitting.
In his latest video, he was seen enjoying a fresh iced vanilla latte that he made in his quarantine room, thanks to an espresso machine he has acquired.

“I can sulk in my bed and just be sad and be like, ‘Wow, I’m stuck here for six and a half weeks,’ or I can be like, ‘OK, they’re letting me do all these things, we’re being well taken care of, we’re being fed,’ and just kind of start counting down,” he said. “If I’m not positive, I mean, what’s the point?”

That positive mindset may be key, given that Rosmarin plans to stay at the Omaha facility throughout the 42-day incubation period for the virus. So far, he said, he has tested negative and is feeling good.

Health officials in the U.S. have encouraged those in quarantine to remain in the medical facilities until that time expires, but have said that patients can leave and self-isolate at home if they so choose.
“It’s the best decision to make for me and for my family and friends and just for the general public,” he said about the decision to stay indoors until the end of June.
Like Brantly, he added that his staff has been “amazing,” meeting his every need at any time.
Carl Goldman, who spent a month in Omaha after contracting Covid-19 during a voyage on the Diamond Princess cruise ship in February 2020, also had a similar experience.
After quarantining on the ship for two weeks, he and about 300 other Americans were flown back to the U.S. It was on the flight that he came down with the deadly virus.
For 10 days, Goldman was housed in the biocontainment unit at UNMC, which he said felt “like a scene out of the movie ‘The Andromeda Strain.’”

The 20-by-15-foot “surrealistic” room was double-sealed, and everyone who entered had to wear a hazmat suit. Doctors did not administer medication, but he was constantly served Gatorade. The blue was better than the purple, he said.

He passed the time by blogging about his experiences and by pacing back and forth in his tiny room, recording 10,000 steps a day. While he could have spent all of his time doing media interviews, he said he allowed himself only three per day.

Once his symptoms subsided, he was transferred to another room, and at least 50 people lined the hallways and cheered him on during the move. He stayed there in a room similar to Rosmarin’s for 20 more days until he left the Nebraska establishment in mid-March 2020 — even celebrating his birthday with cupcakes and balloons from the staff.
He didn’t get to order takeout, but in one interview at the time, he mentioned he wanted a steak from Omaha Steaks. The company’s owner happened to be watching and later delivered steaks for Goldman.
“They cooked Omaha Steaks for everybody, all the people, everything,” he recalled. “So, we got to really have a lot of steak, a feast.”
Now, over six years later, he still remembers his time in Omaha fondly and hopes that the ship passengers currently quarantined “look at the glass half full instead of half empty.”

“Store your memories, because they will be very memorable, but realize that stress is probably the worst thing that you could add to your body right now,” he told NBC News. “Think of it as a long-term vacation. That’s one way to look at it.”

title: Sen. Lindsey Graham says status quo in the Strait of Hormuz is ‘hurting us all’
summary: Sen. Lindsey Graham, R-S.C., called for more U.S. military action in Iran, saying the ongoing stalemate in the war is “hurting us all.”
story: Sen. Lindsey Graham, R-S.C., on Sunday called for more U.S. military action in Iran.
Subscribe to read this story ad-free
Get unlimited access to ad-free articles and exclusive content.

“I think the status quo is hurting us all. The longer the [Strait of Hormuz] is closed, the more we try to pursue a deal that never happens, the stronger Iran gets,” Graham told NBC News’ “Meet the Press.”

Graham added that so far in the Iran conflict, “there’s nothing to suggest that the people in charge now are any different in terms of the regime’s goal to terrorize the world, destroy Israel, come after us.”
He called on President Donald Trump to “weaken them further.”
“What President Trump has done has been amazing militarily, but there’s more targets to be had, and there’s things we can do to hurt [Iran],” the South Carolina senator said.
Graham’s comments come amid a pause in negotiations between the U.S. and Iran, which have not yet led to a deal to end the war.

And the ongoing closure of the Strait of Hormuz, a crucial maritime passageway for oil tankers, has sent global oil prices soaring, raising gas prices for Americans, too. The average price of unleaded gas in the U.S. is up 51% since the U.S. first took military action against Iran more than two months ago.
“Here’s the good news,” Graham told “Meet the Press” moderator Kristen Welker. “Gas prices will come down when you put Iran in a box.”
Iran’s foreign minister said Friday that the nation’s leaders have “no trust” in American negotiators.
His comments came the same day Trump returned to the U.S. from a trip to China, where he met with Chinese President Xi Jinping in a multiday summit.
The U.S. president told Fox News’ Bret Baier that he didn’t ask Chinese leaders for “help” regarding Iran.

“[Xi] agrees with me that he wants to see it end. He’d like to see it end. He would like to help,” Trump said. “If he wants help, that’s great, but we don’t need help. And you know the problem with help: When somebody helps you, they always want something on the other side. That’s the way help works.”

The president also faced criticism last week for saying that he’s considering Americans’ financial situations “not even a little bit” when negotiating an end to the Iran war. The comments sparked fears among Republicans over the upcoming midterm elections.
Graham dismissed these concerns about the midterms, telling Welker, “It’s worth losing my job. If I had to give my job up to make sure Iran would never have a nuclear weapon, I would do it.”

The senator’s comments echoed what Trump told Baier last week, saying, “I’m not going to let the election determine what’s going to happen with respect to Iran, because they cannot have a nuclear weapon.”

The president also said he knew his actions in Iran would “screw up” polling numbers for Republicans running for re-election in the midterms, as some GOP lawmakers grapple with how closely they should align with Trump on the campaign trail.

“I said to my people, ‘I hate to do this to you, but I’m going to screw up your numbers for a little while, because we have to stop the nuclear weapon stuff with Iran, because they’re nuts,’” Trump told Baier last week.

title: ‘Love Is Blind’ Season 4 Stars Chelsea Griffin and Kwame Appiah Announce Divorce After 4 Years of Marriage
summary: “Love Is Blind” Season 4 stars Chelsea Griffin and Kwame Appiah announced that they are divorcing after four years of marriage.
story: “Love Is Blind” Season 4 stars Chelsea Griffin and Kwame Appiah have shared that they are separating after four years of marriage.

On May 15, Griffin and Appiah uploaded a somber black-and-white picture of them in a joint Instagram post with a caption that read, “Life Update,” and included a mending heart emoji. The next two slides in the post were separate statements confirming that they are getting a divorce.

Appiah wrote in her statement, “Last week would have marked our fourth wedding anniversary. Out of respect for the life we shared, I wanted to address this moment directly with the community that has supported us over the years. It’s heartbreaking to share that our marriage is coming to an end.”
She continued, “This is not the outcome I hoped for, and I entered this relationship with deep love, commitment, and the intention of building a lasting life together.”
Griffin said that she and Appiah have realized that their lives are taking them in different directions.

“I poured my whole heart into this marriage and wanted it to work until the very end, but lasting marriages require more than love alone,” she added. “I remain grateful for the life, memories, and experiences we shared. I don’t believe any chapter of our lives is wasted, each one shapes us, teaches us, and becomes part of our personal journey.”

Griffin said that she is still hopeful for her future, just like she was the first day she stepped into the pods on Season 4 of the Netflix reality dating show. She said she hopes to find clarity and healing. At the end of her post, she thanked fans for their kindness and support over the years.
As for Appiah, he expressed a similar grateful sentiment in his statement.

“Last week would have been our wedding anniversary. Unfortunately, we’re taking this moment to announce that after 4 years together, we’re going our separate ways. This isn’t something either of us ever imagined, and it’s been one of the hardest decisions to make,” he began.

Appiah added that he and Griffin “built a life filled with memories, laughter, and love that I’ll always be grateful for. We will always have respect and care for each other in everything we shared. We’re eternally grateful for all the love, support and purpose we gained during our time together.”
He also echoed Griffin’s explanation that the two are divorcing because their life goals do not align.
“Thank you all for the many years of support. Much love to you all,” he concluded.

Appiah and Griffin hit it off in the pods and he penned an original song for her, called “Real Love,” that he sang before he proposed. Although the two faced some challenging moments, they decided to say “I do” and became one of three couples in Season 4 to get married.

After the show, Appiah and Griffin kept fans updated by frequently sharing silly videos together on Instagram. They also appeared in the audience as two of the many alumni that attended the “Love Is Blind” Season 10 reunion in March.

Appiah and Griffin join the following couples who have split after getting married on the show: Danielle Ruhl and Nick Tompson; Jarrette Jones and Iyanna McNeely; Colleen Reed and Matt Bolton; Alexa Alfia and Brennon Lemieux; Lydia Velez Gonzalez and James “Milton” Johnson IV; Tyler Francis and Ashley Adionser; and Amber Morrison and Jordan Faeth.

title: Before His Death, Eric Dane Said ALS Was ‘A Hard Thing’ For Him and His Loved Ones to Live With
summary: Eric Dane reflects on the difficulties of ALS and remaining positive in a preview clip of the documentary short “Ring Every Bell.”
story: Eric Dane hoped to fight his battle with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) with “dignity and grace.”
The actor, known for his “Grey’s Anatomy” and “Euphoria” roles, died at 53 on Feb. 19, 2026, 10 months after revealing his ALS diagnosis.
Before his death, Dane chronicled his final advocacy efforts in the upcoming  I AM ALS documentary short titled “Ring Every Bell” to remind those diagnosed with ALS to “keep going.”
In TODAY.com’s exclusive clip, Dane got candid while reflecting on his battle with ALS and sharing how he hoped to stay positive for those around him.
“It’s a hard thing to live with and it’s a hard thing for the...” Dane said, getting visibly emotional. “It’s a hard thing for the people around me.”
Dane added that those closest to him can’t always help and “they feel helpless.”

“And this is all new to me, this is uncharted territory,” he continued, adding that he was learning about living with ALS as his symptoms progressed. “I’m just trying to, like, walk through this thing with as much grace and dignity as I can so I can show my kids, this is how you do this.”
Dane shared daughters Billie, 16, and Georgia, 14, with wife Rebecca Gayheart. The actor hoped to maintain his optimism so they could better handle life’s unfortunate events long after he was gone.
In the clip, the actor noted that there is a grieving process to his diagnosis but stressed that “it’s also important not to indulge in it.”
Because, he added, at the end of the day, “You just got to keep moving forward.”

The documentary short’s title, “Ring Every Bell,” was chosen because it was a recurring phrase Dane “used to capture the spirit and urgency of his advocacy throughout his ALS journey,” a press release from I AM ALS, a nonprofit organization, said.
Before his death, Dane was able to watch a preliminary cut of “Ring Every Bell” and “was visibly moved and encouraged the team with a simple message: ‘Keep going,’” the organization shared.
He also made it his mission to raise awareness for ALS, partnering with the nonprofit’s Push For Progress initiative and securing $1 billion in federal funding for ALS over the next three years.
“Ring Every Bell,” produced by the nonprofit and filmmaker Christopher Burke, will be released on Monday, May 18, on I AM ALS’ website, YouTube channel and other social channels.

title: See a Pic from ‘Off Campus’ Stars’ Chemistry Read, Taken Before They Knew Each Other’s Names
summary: SEe an eElla Bright and Belmont Cameli shared a picture of their first meeting.
story: Chemistry is essential when you’re filming a love story. Luckily, “Off Campus” stars Ella Bright and Belmont Cameli had it in spades when they auditioned for the Amazon Prime series.
The new show, which premiered on May 13, is based on a bestselling book series by Elle Kennedy.
Described as a “college soap” by Amazon Prime, the eight-episode series follows the “unlikely romance” between music major Hannah Wells (Bright) and hockey player Garrett Graham (Cameli).
While appearing on TODAY on May 15, the show’s leading lady and man looked back on the very first moment that they met.
More of ‘Off Campus’

‘Off Campus’ Ends on a Twisty Cliffhanger. It’s a Mystery Even to the Show’s StarsAll the Differences Between The ‘Off Campus’ Show and Elle Kennedy’s BooksWho Will Lead ‘Off Campus’ Season 2? Ending Leaves Multiple PossibilitiesWho’s Hunter Davenport and How Does He Fit Into the ‘Off Campus’ Books?
‘Off Campus’ Ends on a Twisty Cliffhanger. It’s a Mystery Even to the Show’s Stars
All the Differences Between The ‘Off Campus’ Show and Elle Kennedy’s Books
Who Will Lead ‘Off Campus’ Season 2? Ending Leaves Multiple Possibilities
Who’s Hunter Davenport and How Does He Fit Into the ‘Off Campus’ Books?
TODAY’s Craig Melvin shared an exclusive photo of the star’s staring deeply into each other’s eyes during their first encounter.
“That was the first time you met, huh?” Craig asked.
“That was moments after we met,” Cameli said.
“Yeah, we finished that take and he just went, ‘I’m Bel, by the way. Nice to meet you,’” Bright recalled.
Craig asked the actors about their first impressions of each other after that first chemistry test. Bright described the interaction as “awesome.”
“The scene felt really genuine and real. It was a very intense day,” she said, adding that there were around 28 actors auditioning that day.
“Yeah, there were a ton of actors there. But, I mean, when we got in there, it was readily apparent to us that something special was going on,” Cameli added.
In “Off Campus,” the actors’ characters make a deal to embark on a fake relationship to catch the eye of Hannah’s crush. But things don’t go as they originally planned.

The show, which has already been renewed for a second season, will start filming Season 2 in a few days. When asked what he thinks fans will love about the show, Cameli offered the following response.

“There’s a lot to enjoy. I mean, if you show up for the steamy scenes, that’s what you’re going to get. But the hockey is fantastic in the show. The music, the friendships, the romance, the family. There’s some deeper themes that we explore in, I think. a really meaningful way. There’s a lot for audiences to enjoy,” he said.
Following the success of “Heated Rivalry,” TV series about hockey are certainly having a moment. Caneli and Bright say the two shows’ casts got the chance to meet a few months ago at a party.
“It was really cool to be in the same room as them and chat with them about their experience and ours. They’re great. Yeah, I love those boys. I’m really so stoked for them,” he said.
“Yeah, we’re huge fans,” Bright added.

title: ‘Off Campus’ Showrunner Unpacks Those Big Changes From the Book
summary: What are the changes and differences between the “Off Campus” show and Elle Kennedy’s book, “The Deal”? The show’s creator unpacks it all.
story: Warning: This article contains spoilers for “Off Campus” Season 1.

“Off Campus” Season 1 proves a faithful adaptation of Elle Kennedy’s 2015 hockey romance novel “The Deal” — mostly. Prime Video released all eight episodes of the college-set romance show May 13. But even before its release, dedicated book fans hoped it would honor some the book’s most beloved moments.
Some came true. Some did not.
Belmont Cameli and Ella Bright, who star in “Off Campus” Season 1 as romantic leads Garrett and Hannah, have said they read “The Deal” in preparation for their roles.

“I think all of the changes that we made to put this book on screen were really amazing and really thoughtful changes that I really enjoyed,” Bright tells TODAY.com. “We made it our own in a really special way. I think people can hopefully love both of them in their own individual ways.”
More of ‘Off Campus’

‘Off Campus’ Ends on a Twisty Cliffhanger. It’s a Mystery Even to the Show’s Stars‘Off Campus’ Stars Ella Bright and Belmont Cameli React to Unseen Pic From Their Chemistry ReadWho Will Lead ‘Off Campus’ Season 2? Ending Leaves Multiple PossibilitiesWho’s Hunter Davenport and How Does He Fit Into the ‘Off Campus’ Books?
‘Off Campus’ Ends on a Twisty Cliffhanger. It’s a Mystery Even to the Show’s Stars
‘Off Campus’ Stars Ella Bright and Belmont Cameli React to Unseen Pic From Their Chemistry Read
Who Will Lead ‘Off Campus’ Season 2? Ending Leaves Multiple Possibilities
Who’s Hunter Davenport and How Does He Fit Into the ‘Off Campus’ Books?
Cameli adds that he can’t think of anything from the book that was truly missing in the show.

“In the book, you get to hear everything that these two characters are thinking and feeling at all times, and in a show you don’t have that luxury,” he says. “So we wanted to show some of those things rather than tell.”

“I think (co-showrunners) Louisa (Levy) and Gina (Fattore) and the writing team did a really good job if not perfectly translating something from the book, paying homage, or making it better, more fitting for TV,” he adds.
Read on for all the major differences between “Off Campus” and Kennedy’s book that inspired it all.
Hannah’s Crush, Justin, Is Now a Musician
Both the book and show focus on the titular “deal” between Hannah and Garrett: She will tutor him, and he’ll pretend to date her to attract the attention of her crush, Justin Kohl (Josh Heuston).

In the book, Justin is a football player who Hannah believes “gives off an intelligent, intense vibe.” So though she’s “not usually into jocks,” her belief in his “hidden depths” draws her attraction.

As Garrett pursues Hannah for studying help, he learns of her crush on Justin. He insists that, due to their shared athletic circle, if Hannah is seen on a date with Garrett, it’ll raise her social currency and desirability in Justin’s eyes.
In the show, however, Justin isn’t a football player, but a musician.
Heuston tells TODAY.com that his character and Garrett have a “similar status on campus.” Both are in the limelight in their respective fields.

“I think when he sees that Garrett is interested in this girl, and she seems genuine and endearing in all these ways, Justin’s like, ‘Oh, this person’s going to be actually liking me for me, not just the college rock star.’”
Elle Kennedy, the author of the books, even approves.

“I’m actually happy with making Justin a musician in the show,” Kennedy told E! News when asked about book-to-show changes. “It was just so unlike her to have a crush on a jock, so I thought it would be really funny.”

“It was a way for Garrett to know who Justin is and why they would be at the same parties because, for the most part, they probably wouldn’t,” she added. “But I really, really enjoyed making Justin the musician.”
Hannah Kisses Logan, Not Dean
After Hannah and Garrett strike up their deal, they soon run into a slight snag: PDA.

Garrett insists that to truly sell their fake relationship, they need to be affectionate with each other in front of other people. When Hannah seems hesitant, he accuses her of being scared, so she kisses him in defiance.

In the books, resident bad boy/player Dean Di Laurentis then walks in on them, after they had previously told all the housemates they were just study buddies. To back up her white lie that it was just kissing practice, she walks up to Dean and kisses him, too.
In the show, Garrett and Hannah’s kiss happens in the gym. It’s not Dean who walks in on them, though — it’s John Logan, played by Antonio Cipriano.
To prove her and Garrett’s kiss didn’t mean anything, she kisses Logan right after.
Showrunner Louisa Levy says that change was made to tee up an important part of Logan’s story told in book two, “The Mistake”: his crush on Hannah.
“That’s obviously such a fun moment in the book,” Levy tells TODAY.com. “We have such limited time, and we felt like that real estate was useful setting up Logan’s crush.”

“We wanted to kind of collapse those two things and make them work with each other,” she adds. “We felt like that one small change, even though it was really fun with Dean in the books, we felt like switching it to Logan helped him and his story more than the kiss with Dean helped him in his.”
Allie and Dean Start Their Relationship Much, Much Earlier
Speaking of Dean, the events of his book — book three, “The Score” — came earlier than expected, as he and his eventual love interest, Hannah’s best friend Allie, start hooking up in Season 1.
Episode 5 ends with a big twist: For months after her breakup with longterm boyfriend Sean, Allie (Mika Abdalla) has been secretly having a rebound fling with Dean (Stephen Kalyn).
This aligns with the events of Kennedy’s book, “The Score.” Like the show, Dean and Allie start sleeping together shortly after her breakup with Sean but keep it a secret from their friends.
They eventually realize they are essentially already in a relationship and decide to make things official after Hannah and Garrett accidentally find out about their arrangement.

The show sets up a less smooth course forward, though. In the show, Allie has the epiphany that she’s jumping immediately into another relationship and decides she wants to prove to herself she can be single. So she gives herself and Dean an “assignment”: sleep with someone else.

In the final moments of Season 1, Dean tells her he couldn’t follow through with it and confesses his feelings for her. But Allie admits she did complete the assignment. And, unbeknownst to her, the person she slept with is none other than Dean’s nemesis: Hunter Davenport.
Garrett Breaks Up with Hannah
In both Season 1 and “The Deal,” Garrett and Hannah fall victim to the classic romantic trope of the third-act breakup.
After Garrett gets in a fight on the ice for Hannah, they call it quits, but the circumstances differ between the book and show.

In the book, Garrett’s dad confronts Hannah after the fight and accuses her of distracting his son and jeopardizing his future. He gives her an ultimatum: break up with Garrett or he’ll financially cut his son off.

She goes through with it and tries to cut off contact, but Garrett refuses to give up. He enacts a campus “hands-off” ban, promising his wrath if another guy pursues Hannah. After she confronts him about it, they kiss, and she shares the real reason she ended things. They get back together after he reveals that he has a trust fund out of his dad’s control.
But in the show, it’s Garrett who breaks up with Hannah.
Levy says this change was made because of the evolving world of college athletics.

“Because the books came out over a decade ago, we now have different NCAA laws where athletes can earn money themselves,” Levy says. “It just didn’t feel credible in today’s landscape. So we were trying to find a way around that.”
Garrett decides to break up with Hannah after the fight because he’s scared he will turn out like his abusive father.

“It also just felt so much more organic that Garrett would break up with Hannah because of his inner fear that he would become his dad, which, of course, he would not, but we buy that he would believe that,” Levy adds.

Hannah is heartbroken. Garrett eventually opens up to his best friend John Logan about his father’s abuse and rediscovers why he loves hockey. At the last minute, Hannah decides to perform at the pop showcase, and Garrett and the team sits front row in support. After, outside the auditorium, he asks for her back, and they share a kiss.
No ‘Hands-Off’ Campus Ban?
Because Garrett is the one who broke up with Hannah in the show, the circumstances of his “hands-off” campus ban had to change, too.
So it all turns out to be a misunderstanding.
In Episode 8, after their breakup, Hannah is working her shift at the diner. When she approaches a table of guys to take their order, they barely look her in the eye and ask for a different server.
Later, she hears of the “hands-off” rule and storms into the Briar hockey locker room to confront Garrett.

In the book, Garrett responds to her accusation with a boastful, “Of course I did.” But in the show, he immediately denies it. Turns out, the “campus ban” was just a rumor that began when another hockey player misinterpreted a comment from Dean.
“Look, I would never tell anyone to stay away from you. You should be with whoever you want to be,” Garrett tells Hannah.

While Levy maintains Garrett and Hannah’s breakup feels more current and natural in the show, “the casualty of that is the ‘hands-off law’ feels much worse coming from Garrett if he broke up with Hannah.”

“We were trying to find a way to keep this fan-favorite moment from the book, while not making it like a problem for Garrett,” Levy says. “So we came up with a solution that still honors the original intent but makes Garrett not completely terrible for doing this after breaking up with Hannah.”

title: Who Will Lead ‘Off Campus’ Season 2? Ending Leaves Multiple Possibilities
summary: Warning: This article contains spoilers for the ending of “Off Campus” Season 1.
story: Warning: This article contains spoilers for the ending of “Off Campus” Season 1.
Like Elle Kennedy’s beloved book universe on which it’s based, Prime Video’s “Off Campus” series follows Hannah Wells and Garrett Graham, the leads of the 2015 book “The Deal” that started it all.
But will Season 2 follow book two?

“Off Campus” showrunner Louisa Levy tells TODAY.com she can’t reveal the couple at the center of the show’s second season, which was ordered in February, well before all eight episodes of Season 1 premiered May 13.
“Soon,” she promises in the April interview.
That leaves Season 1’s chaotic ending as viewers’ primary clue for what Season 2 might bring, and there’s seemingly two possibilities.
More of ‘Off Campus’

‘Off Campus’ Ends on a Twisty Cliffhanger. It’s a Mystery Even to the Show’s Stars‘Off Campus’ Stars Ella Bright and Belmont Cameli React to Unseen Pic From Their Chemistry ReadAll the Differences Between The ‘Off Campus’ Show and Elle Kennedy’s BooksWho’s Hunter Davenport and How Does He Fit Into the ‘Off Campus’ Books?
‘Off Campus’ Ends on a Twisty Cliffhanger. It’s a Mystery Even to the Show’s Stars
‘Off Campus’ Stars Ella Bright and Belmont Cameli React to Unseen Pic From Their Chemistry Read
All the Differences Between The ‘Off Campus’ Show and Elle Kennedy’s Books
Who’s Hunter Davenport and How Does He Fit Into the ‘Off Campus’ Books?
The sophomore outing could stay true to the books and follow the love story between John Logan (Antonio Cipriano) and Grace Ivers from “The Mistake.”

Or the show could continue its leap forward by further spotlighting the budding romance between Dean Di Laurentis (Stephen Kalyn) and Allie Hayes (Mika Abdalla), which, in the book series, doesn’t even begin until the third novel, “The Score.”
Read on for the case for both, and what to know about their stories.
John Logan and Grace Ivers, ‘The Mistake’

Garrett’s (Belmont Cameli) best friend in the house of four hockey players is defenseman John Logan. He’s also the star of Kennedy’s sequel to “The Deal,” titled “The Mistake,” which published in April 2015.

The book reveals that Logan has a crush on Garrett’s love interest, Hannah Wells (Ella Bright). The novel also sets up his starkly different prospects compared to Garrett: Instead of an on-lock NHL future, Logan faces intense pressure to join the family business after graduation and care for his alcoholic parent instead of pursue professional hockey.

So far in the screen adaptation, not much of Logan’s story from the novel has been told. Viewers learn about his crush on Hannah and his complicated family life. In Episode 5, he goes to visit his mother in rehab on Thanksgiving.
Looking ahead to Logan’s story, Cipriano tells TODAY.com he’s looking forward to showing the “light” of his character.

“In the first season, he’s going through it a little bit. There’s a lot coming up for him. There’s so many things happening that may make him a little bit stressed out,” Cipriano says. “For us to be able to see someone come into his life and light it up a little bit, I think that’s going to be really rewarding for Logan.”
“As an actor, that’s going to be really cool to get to play the dark side before you get to see the light,” he adds.

“The Mistake” begins very shortly after the events of “The Deal.” Logan, in his junior year, almost hooks up with freshman Grace Ivers, but a “thoughtless mistake pushes her away,” per the book synopsis. The next school year, Logan spends his senior year trying to get sophomore Grace to forgive him.
Grace Ivers does not appear in Season 1, however her name is mentioned briefly in Episode 6 when she’s announced as the winner of an auction prize.
“We wanted to just nod to, ‘Don’t worry we’re getting there,’ to our fans,” Levy says of the Easter egg moment.
While Logan’s story may seem like it’s on the back burner, some off screen news increased the likelihood that the show could still stick to book order.
Prime Video revealed April 30 that it has cast India Fowler, known for “Fear Street: Prom Queen,” to play Grace in “Off Campus” Season 2.
Cipriano revealed the news to Fowler in a video shared to social media.
“I wish that, you know, we could work together sometime soon,” he said after seemingly teeing her up for rejection.
“How does next month sound to you?” he continued. “Would you maybe want to come be Grace and do Season 2 with me?”
Dean Di Laurentis and Allie Hayes, ‘The Score’
Unlike Logan’s story, several big moments from Dean’s book have already been brought to life on screen.

His love interest is Allie Hayes, Hannah’s roommate and best friend in both Season 1 and “The Deal.” Allie’s an aspiring actor in a long-term relationship with her boyfriend, Sean, who belittles her big screen ambitions, making her realize they are fundamentally incompatible after several short-term breakups.
Starting with Episode 2, Allie also draws the eye of resident player Dean, known for his promiscuity and staunch anti-relationship stance. She is shown rebuffing him.

But in a twist ending to Episode 5, viewers learn that after her split with Sean, she and Dean struck up a hookup arrangement, culminating in an intimate meetup in New York City over Thanksgiving break.
“It felt very organic,” Levy tells TODAY.com of jumping ahead to the events of “The Score.” “We have Allie in Season 1 anyway. We have Dean there, of course.”

“I really love that their story is a secret at the beginning. And so I thought what better way to platform that than to keep it a secret from the audience,” Levy adds. “And I think if we did it any later it might not be a secret.”

Half of Episode 6 is dedicated entirely to rewinding the clock on how Allie and Dean started hooking up, re-creating several notable beats from their book, “The Score,” which published in January 2016.

Like the side plot of Season 1, “The Score” sees Allie, fresh off a breakup, beginning to hook up with Dean, though she struggles with casual sex and feeling “slutty.” While he helps her embrace her sexuality, she makes him realize he may no longer want sexual freedom and emotional detachment like before.
They continue their hookup arrangement in the book until real feelings start to give way.

Season 1 ends with Dean telling Allie he has feelings for her, but in a major change from the books, she reveals that she hooked up with someone else, their previously agreed upon “assignment” to prove she can be single and unattached.
The final scene of Season 1 reveals that person is none other than Hunter Davenport (Charlie Evans), another hockey player and familiar name to book fans.
While Hunter and Dean are friends in the books, the show establishes a yet-to-be explained longstanding feud. After Dean learns of Hunter and Allie’s tryst, he and Hunter fight in the bar.
The last shot before the credits is Allie’s shocked reaction.
“I think that’s her biggest fear realized,” Abdalla tells TODAY.com. “She’s just so afraid of hurting the people that she maybe loves and just doesn’t know it yet.”
“I do think Allie has feelings for Dean, and I think hearing that, it’s just kind of like a, ‘What did I do?’ world crashing down around me, kind of realization of her big fear,” she adds.
While Allie and Dean’s story is already well underway, Levy confirms to TODAY.com they will still get their own starring season.
“We have lots of story to tell with them still,” Levy says. “We’ve got lots to do with them, we’ve got lots to do with all the characters.”

title: Who’s Hunter Davenport and How Does He Fit Into the ‘Off Campus’ Books?
summary: Warning: This article contains spoilers for the ending of “Off Campus” Season 1.The very last scene of “Off Campus” Season 1 tees up a complicated future for the Briar University hockey team and a big change from the books by introducing a character whose story isn’t told until the seventh novel of the Briar U literary universe.“Off Campus” is based on the book series of the same name by Elle Kennedy.
story: Warning: This article contains spoilers for the ending of “Off Campus” Season 1.

The very last scene of “Off Campus” Season 1 tees up a complicated future for the Briar University hockey team and a big change from the books by introducing a character whose story isn’t told until the seventh novel of the Briar U literary universe.

“Off Campus” is based on the book series of the same name by Elle Kennedy. The first season follows captain Garrett Graham (Belmont Cameli) and his love story with aspiring composer Hannah Wells (Ella Bright).

Keeping with the conventions of romance shows before it, Season 1 ends with Garrett and Hannah’s happily ever after. But Garrett’s status on the ice doesn’t have as hopeful of an outlook. In the Season 1 finale, he’s deemed ineligible to play college hockey due to a video of him seemingly practicing with a professional team.
More on ‘Off Campus’

‘Off Campus’ Ends on a Twisty Cliffhanger. It’s a Mystery Even to the Show’s Stars‘Off Campus’ Stars Ella Bright and Belmont Cameli React to Unseen Pic From Their Chemistry ReadAll the Differences Between The ‘Off Campus’ Show and Elle Kennedy’s BooksWho Will Lead ‘Off Campus’ Season 2? Ending Leaves Multiple Possibilities
‘Off Campus’ Ends on a Twisty Cliffhanger. It’s a Mystery Even to the Show’s Stars
‘Off Campus’ Stars Ella Bright and Belmont Cameli React to Unseen Pic From Their Chemistry Read
All the Differences Between The ‘Off Campus’ Show and Elle Kennedy’s Books
Who Will Lead ‘Off Campus’ Season 2? Ending Leaves Multiple Possibilities
It also means Briar University’s record resets to zero eligible wins.

“The stakes at hand is that they have to go on to win or tie, they can’t lose any longer,” Cameli tells TODAY.com. “We’ll have to find out how it all pans for them. If Garrett returns to the team, where he ends up, how the team performs, that all remains to be seen, even to me.”

Without Garrett on the ice, his teammates are trying strengthen their roster by trying to recruit another player — Hunter Davensport (Charlie Evans). It’s revealed that Hunter was previously offered a spot on Briar’s team but turned it down and that Dean (Stephen Kalyn) has a longstanding issue with him. Viewers later learn that Hunter went to high school with Dean’s younger sister.

The final scene of Season 1 sets up plenty of conflict for Season 2, after Hunter is revealed to be the guy Allie (Mika Abdalla) hooked up with in an effort to prove she’s not jumping into another relationship with Dean. Dean finds out about their tryst right after telling Allie he has feelings for her, which leads Dean to throw fists at Hunter as the rest of the hockey team attempt to break it up.

So it seems Hunter Davenport arrived just in time to cause so many problems for this “Off Campus” crew. But who is he, exactly? Well, Hunter is in fact a character from Kennedy’s books, but he’s not from “Off Campus” — and he definitely never hooks up with Allie.
Rather, he’s one of the main characters spotlighted in Kennedy’s “Off Campus” spinoff series, titled “Briar U.”

“Elle Kennedy built a universe for us,” showrunner Louisa Levy says of jumping ahead in the book series. “And if Amazon will let me, I would love to keep playing in this sandbox for all of the books.”
Who Is Hunter Davenport?

Hunter Davenport is a hockey protégé a few years younger than the core cast of “Off Campus.” He’s first mentioned in Logan’s book, the second in the series called “The Mistake,” as a freshman to the team. In Dean’s book, “The Score,” he takes Hunter under his wing.
But Hunter really takes center ice in the “Briar U” series, which follows a new set of hockey players and their love interests.

The first “Briar U” book, “The Chase,” stars Dean’s younger sister, Summer, as she transfers to Briar and falls for her new housemate and hockey player, Colin “Fitz” Fitzgerald. While her heart lies with Fitz, she first leads on another one of her housemates who has a crush on her: Hunter.
Temporarily heartbroken by Fitz and Summer’s relationship, Hunter eventually finds love in the third “Briar U” book, “The Play.”
What Happens in Hunter’s Book, ‘The Play’?
“The Play” sees Hunter, now captain of the hockey team, decide to swear after sex and dating after a previous hookup led to an on-ice scuffle with a rival player.
Then, he’s paired for a project with a classmate named Demi Davis. She’s got a boyfriend though. Well, until she soon does not.
Read the full synopsis over on Elle Kennedy’s website.

title: ‘Off Campus’ Ends on a Twisty Cliffhanger. It’s a Mystery Even to the Show’s Stars
summary: It’s been two days since the internet’s latest hockey romance obsession, “Off Campus,” hit streaming.
story: It’s been two days since the internet’s latest hockey romance obsession, “Off Campus,” hit streaming. The show’s leads — Ella Bright and Belmont Cameli — haven’t really had a second to process it, and they won’t anytime soon.
“We’re going back to Vancouver in a couple days to shoot Season 2,” Cameli tells TODAY.com May 15 right after his and Bright’s joint appearance on the show.

“Off Campus,” based on the series of books by Elle Kennedy, premiered all eight episodes May 13 on Prime Video. The duo star as hockey captain Garrett Graham and singer-songwriter Hannah Wells, who fall in love after striking up a mutually beneficial fake dating scheme.
Based on the first book in the series, 2015’s “The Deal,” that titular deal involves Hannah tutoring Garrett, while he pretends to date her to attract the attention of her campus crush.

The show was renewed for a second season in February, months before Season 1 debuted. And after Episode 8 ended with a cliffhanger, through the introduction of a notable name who doesn’t star in the books until the “Off Campus” spinoff series, “Briar U” — specifically book No. 3, “The Play” — fans are already clamoring for more.
Below, Cameli and Bright discuss with TODAY.com that “mic drop” ending, react to “Heated Rivalry” comparisons and recall meeting Kennedy.
This interview was lightly edited for clarity.
Add TODAY to Google
Add TODAY to Google
Belmont Cameli: I mean, this is crazy.
Ella Bright: It’s insane. We’re at the end of the press tour. It’s been a very emotional few weeks. We’re just so grateful and excited and can’t believe it’s finally out. It’s really crazy.

BC: We’ve been keeping very busy. I mean, we’ve had so much press, and we’ve been doing it all together, and it’s been really, really fun. ... We’re going to stay busy, because we’re going back to Vancouver in a couple days to shoot Season 2, so it’s kind of nice, like we’ll all be together. And we’ve been together for this moment.

BC: When we were making it, we knew that it had an already built-in fervent fanbase. But when we were making it, it felt like any other job. Our team had a really strong vision for the show, and it was so collaborative, that we didn’t really feel too much pressure. We just felt really grateful to bring these iconic characters alive.

EB: And be given the space to make it our own. But now being on the press tour, we’ve met so many people who have read the books or are fans, with all the countries we’ve been to. It’s been really crazy, and that’s kind of been the realization, I think. But as far as pressure goes, it’s just been actually really lovely. And everyone’s been so supportive.
BC: Elle came to set one day, or maybe for like a week? She was there for maybe a couple days.
EB: Yeah.
BC: And it was so fun to have her there. I mean, to watch her reaction of stepping into the world that she built in her imagination was just fascinating and so cute.
EB: She sat in on the table read, I think we were reading Episode 4. She’s awesome. I’m such a big fan of her work.

EB: She just showed a lot of support. She was getting some screeners and some clips of the show while we were filming, and just texting us, usually just being like, “It looks great. Really proud and happy about it.” That was always so encouraging.
BC: Her approval means the world to us.
EB: Yeah, like “Thank god.”

BC: I think in some ways, definitely. I was just happy for “Heated Rivalry” from, like, a TV success story, even separate from it being in the same lane. For a production like that to grab hold of an audience that large is success in any context for TV. So it was really cool to see, and obviously, it does play in our arena.
So having “Heated Rivalry” and the Olympics, you have the playoffs now, it’s just kind of been one thing after another. All hockey press is good hockey press, in my opinion.

EB: I mean, I’m from London. Never heard of ice hockey. So it’s really funny for it all to just be happening at the same time. But like Bel said, I’m such a big fan of the show, and it was really hilarious. Then seeing some people make comparisons, (it feels) crazy to just be in that conversation. It was really funny.

EB: I was just happy for them from just, like, an isolated standpoint. They were lovely. We got to meet them at a party we went to, and they had some really sweet advice, just in general, about just enjoying this.
BC: I wasn’t too intimidated ... I mean, we’ve just been taking this whole thing one day at a time, regardless of the “Heated Rivalry” of it, like this experience has been very individual to us.

EB: I loved Hannah’s quirkiness and bubbliness and her confidence in who she is as a person. And I feel like I see myself in that a lot, like I am very clumsy and insane. (Laughs.) I really related to her on that level. I thought her story was really beautiful and important, and I felt very connected to it right away. And the “deal” scene in the end of the pilot that was when I really realized I really wanted this role so bad.

BC: I think for me, Garrett’s dedication to his sport is something that really resonated. The way I approach my work is very Garrett Graham, in his, like, concerted, universal effort to just be the best he can be. I was excited to step into his shoes for that reason.

I thought Garrett’s story had a lot of meat on the bone and had some really important elements to tell as storytellers. So I was just excited, kind of across the board. But that’s the first thing that stuck out to me, was his competitive drive and his dedication.

BC: So for Garrett, his walls have been so high and so fortified against sharing his story and being seen. Obviously, we see him share his story with Hannah, and that’s a big step for him, but he needs to let in his best friends, too, in order to be supported and and to appreciate the sport that he inherently does love.

It’s easy to build up a resentment for something that you’ve been doing for since you were a really small child, especially when it comes to athletics. Especially when it comes to the dynamics with his father and the domestic violence that he’s experienced. But the whole reason he loves hockey is because it’s about being on a team. So opening up to Logan really unlocks for him his love for hockey. It’s a big moment, and so I’m excited to see in future seasons his relationship with hockey blossom just as much as with Hannah and with the guys.
BC: I don’t think I clocked that Garrett clocked it in Season 1.
EB: I don’t think so.
BC: And I certainly didn’t play it that way. But I mean, we’ll see.
BC: That is still true.
EB: This is true — well, no, I’ve read — yeah, I’ve read the two — well, I’ve read the next few books. (Laughs).
BC: Oh, you have?
EB: Yes, I have. ... When we got the second season. Because I’m very superstitious. I was like, “I don’t want to read it until ...”

But yeah, when we were filming, I’d only read the first, so when we found out about this Hunter Davenport character, I was very confused. But it’s the “Briar U” series. ... But he’s in “The Score” a lot.

BC: Well, you know, it kind of was and it wasn’t. Because there is a whole backstory that the audience is not made aware of, and in some ways, we were not made privy to either, to protect that kind of compelling, mic-drop moment for us as performers, too.
We’re supposed to be incredulous as it’s happening. We’re not supposed to know what’s happening, so it wasn’t explained to us thoroughly.
And we’ll flesh that out more when we get the new episodes.
EB: What was fun about the filming process is we would get two episodes at the time. So we didn’t know how the show ended until a month before.
BC: Right.
EB: So it was like reading it and watching it at the same time. It was kind of like the experience for the audience. It was just like a fun, plot-twist moment.

BC: Ultimately, no. I have so much confidence in our whole cast. I’m really excited to watch whoever’s up next and whoever may be after them step into these shoes. Because, I mean, everybody is really prepared for this moment and deserves it entirely.
EB: Absolutely.

title: A Main Character Is Fated to Die in the ‘Outlander’ Finale. What Happens?
summary: Does Jamie really die in the outlander finale? Here’s what happens to Jamie and Claire at the end of Outlander.
story: The “Outlander” Season 8 premiere foreshadowed that a main character would die.

Not just any main character — but Jamie Fraser, Sam Heughan’s beloved Scottish highlander who falls for Claire Beauchamp, a time-traveling nurse from the 1940s played by Caitriona Balfe. The epic Starz series, based on Diana Gabaldon’s novels of the same name, ran from 2014 to 2026.
The series finale, which aired on May 15, finally shows what happens, and if our beloved hero’s time has come to an end in more ways than one.
What happened and what has the cast said about the end of the series? Here’s everything you need to know.
What Happens in the ‘Outlander’ Finale?
The “Outlander” Season 8 premiere contains a shocking prophecy. Jamie and Claire’s daughter Brianna (Sophie Skelton) brings a book back from the future, where she’d been with her family (long story).

The book is written by Frank (Tobias Menzies), Claire’s first husband, an American historian who wrote about the Scottish influence of the American revolution. In the book, Frank says that Jamie would die in battle.

The finale follows Jamie and Claire as they prepare for his impending demise. Alongside his men, Jamie rides to King’s Mountain to prepare for a battle with Major Patrick Ferguson. Jamie makes a few requests of Claire before he leaves, making it clear that he’s not intending to return.
During the battle, Jamie comes face to face with Ferguson and viewers think he’s about to surrender. Then, Ferguson shoots Jamie.
OK, But Is Jamie Really Dead?
It’s complicated. After Jamie is shot, the episode continues and wraps up a seasons-long mystery involving his ghost.

Jamie’s ghost goes back through Claire’s life, returning to moments from the series premiere, like when Frank sees Jamie’s ghost. Then Jamie steps into the stone circles at Craigh Na Dun, where Claire first time-traveled. He leaves forget-me-not flowers in his wake — the very flowers that Claire was drawn to.
When the camera returns to Jamie and Claire, her hair is bright white, a nod to the prophecy that she would come into her full healing powers when her hair was white.
Then comes the unexpected twist. Suddenly, they both gasp for air — then the episode ends. The ending implies Claire was able to heal her husband.

“Outlander” showrunner Matthew B. Roberts spoke with Entertainment Weekly about the show’s ending, but wouldn’t confirm whether Jamie survived. He did, however, confirm that both Claire and Jamie’s eyes opened at the end of the episode.
When pressed on the subject, Roberts offered the following explanation of how Jamie could have survived, despite what Frank’s book said.

“Let me put it this way: Say you were documenting that battle, and Jamie got shot on Wednesday, and everybody mourned his death on Wednesday night. You, as the historian, went back to wherever you were from, Philadelphia perhaps, and wrote the story on Thursday that he died. Frank would have read that dispatch. He would have dug down in history and found that this man, in fact, died on this day,” he said.
What the Stars and Author Have Said About the ‘Outlander’ Finale
The “Outlander” series finale leaves a lot up to interpretation. Balfe, who plays Claire, told Entertainment Weekly she’s also confused about the show’s ending.
“I’m not sure that I fully understand the ending ... as to what happens when they’re lying on the stone, I don’t know,” she said.
When Claire gasped at the end of the episode, Balfe said she acted that scene as though her character had died momentarily.

“That’s how I was sort of playing it. He is her home. It’s very Romeo and Juliet, almost, they die together on this altar or this stone. It’s what you hope happens in Romeo and Juliet. We get to change the Shakespearean ending,” she said.

In an interview with Parade, “Outlander” author Diana Gabaldon said she made some suggestions to the showrunner ahead of the finale. One was that Claire “refuse to be separated from Jamie.” She also said the finale should “fade out on two entwined and glowing lovers without further action or comment and go black.”
Gabaldon has said “A Blessing for the Warrior Going Out,” book 10 of the series, will be her last “Outlander” book. She is currently writing the novel.

title: Harry Connick Jr. Is About to Step Foot Into Carnegie Hall for the First Time. He’ll Have One Person On His Mind
summary: Legendary musician, Harry Connick Jr., opens up about honoring his late mother, playing Carnegie Hall for the first time and parenting his three daughters.
story: It’s hard to imagine a stage that legendary musician Harry Connick Jr. hasn’t performed on. But there’s an outstanding venue on his bucket list, and he’s about to cross it off.
“My mother used to say she could die happy knowing that I played at Carnegie Hall,” Connick said on TODAY May 11.

When Connick was 10 years old, his mother was diagnosed with ovarian cancer. Knowing that performing at the New York landmark was something she dreamed of, Connick called the auditorium from his home in New Orleans and said, “My name’s Harry Connick, and I’m a piano player. I want to know if I can play there.”
He was told that he had reached the box office and, no, they couldn’t help him. But at least he could tell his mom that he tried.

To this day, Connick tells TODAY.com that he’s never stepped foot in the concert hall. But on May 22, he’ll make good on his mother’s wish and perform at Carnegie Hall, a once-in-a-lifetime show that Connick says has been decades in the making.

“It’s almost legendary in my mind, because my mother being from New York and being a big music fan, that was the epitome of a performance space to her. So, I decided long ago that I would never go inside until it was a special occasion,” Connick says.

“It’s been 45 years since I’ve wanted to play there and so I decided if I’m going to make it the first time, I’m not going to rehearse there, I’m not going to do anything,” he adds. “I’m just going to walk in, walk on the stage and go do it.”
‘My Mother Is Everything to Me’
Connick’s mom, Anita-Francis Livingston Connick, or “Babe” as she was known, would have turned 100 on May 22. She died in 1981, when Connick was 13.

In the years since her death, Connick has become one of the most renowned artists in the world, releasing more than two dozen albums and winning three Grammys, including one for “When Harry Met Sally ...” He’s also acted in blockbuster films like “Independence Day” and “The Iron Giant,” along with the television show “Will & Grace.”
“My mother is everything to me and formed who I am, even in that short amount of time,” he says. “To be able to honor her in this way on that night, it’s not going to get any better than that.”
Connick will take the stage for two concerts at Carnegie Hall on May 22 and 23, shows that were booked nearly seven years ago.
“No performance I’ve ever done will come close to that and no performance after that,” he says. “I mean, it’s impossible.”
Does he expect to be emotional when he walks out on stage for the first time?
“I spent a lot of time earlier in life anticipating feelings before they happen and it didn’t really get me anywhere,” he says.

“If I thought about how I was going to feel, I’d be a wreck all the time because if you think about what I’m doing, for me, it’s impossibly emotional,” he adds. “It’s a night that’s dedicated to my hero and I’ve never gone in that room specifically until her 100th.”
‘Babe: Elaboratio’

Connick, who’s widely known for crooning jazz and big band favorites, says at the concert he’ll be performing “Babe: Elaboratio,” a three-movement orchestral piece he composed in honor of his beloved mom.
“It’s really not a symphony. It’s more of a concerto because it’s for me as a soloist with a full orchestra and a big band,” Connick says.
The title, he explains, represents both his mother’s nickname and a humorous take on the word “elaborate.”
“This whole piece is an elaboration of what I imagined her life to be, because I didn’t know her before I was born, and then I only knew her for 13 years after that,” Connick says.
His mom was “not a fan of two-dollar words,” he recalls.
“She was a lawyer, and she wasn’t into legalese,” he says.
So, in a tongue-and-cheek nod to his mom’s preference for straight-shooting communication, Connick says he asked an editor friend to come up with an elaborate word for, well, elaborate.
The result is “Elaboratio.”
“Which is obnoxious,” he says. “My mother would have probably called it ‘Music for My Mother.’”
But “elaborating” is exactly what he’s doing — not only in the orchestral piece, but also in an upcoming book of the same name, slated for release on Sept. 29.
Between the two, he hopes to tell the story of her life.

“What I wanted to do was write a book going through every measure of the piece,” he says. “Not only telling about stories that my mother told me, but imagining what it would have been like for her, say, as a young woman who moved to Istanbul when she was in her mid 20s, because she had a sad home life, or what it was like being in New York City in the 1930s.”
‘I Just Try and Be the Best Person I Can Be’
Ahead of his Carnegie Hall debut, Connick says he’s trying not to anticipate how’s he going to feel on that night.
“I don’t live my life like that,” he says. “I’ll feel what I feel when I walk out there and if I’m happy or if I’m weeping or if I’m sobbing ... it just doesn’t benefit me to think about that.”
That in-the-moment mindset inspired another project. Connick says he’s written a yet-to-be-released book about mindfulness, encouraged by his three children: Georgia, 30, Kate, 28, and Charlotte, 23.

“People talk about mindfulness, zen and pragmatism and things like that, but they don’t really tell you what to do with that information. Like, you can sit here and be mindful, but what does that really mean? How does that manifest this stuff in terms of, in my case, creativity and productivity? So, the book is about specifically that,” Connick says.
When it comes to parenting, he says he and wife Jill Goodacre try to avoid offering advice.

“I’ve never been a fan of receiving unsolicited advice,” he says. “My parents were all about the discovery process and if I ran into trouble, they would be there, but words like ‘you should’ never really come out of my mouth.”
Instead, he and Goodacre want their daughters to “do their thing.”

“What we find is that we’re much more in tune with them as a result of that because, for me, if somebody’s saying, ‘This is what you should do,’ I’m not going to go to that person. I don’t want to hear that,” he says. “They’re extremely bright, very strong-willed with definitive perspectives on everything.”
“I just try to be the best person I can be and love them as much as I can,” he adds. “And I find that the decisions that they make have been pretty incredible.”

title: Shakira Gets the Party Started With Official FIFA World Cup 2026 Song ‘Dai Dai’
summary: Shakira is once again getting fans excited for the FIFA World Cup with her new song, “Dai Dai.” Find out more about the song and the opening ceremonies.
story: Shakira is once again getting fans excited for the FIFA World Cup.
After previously teasing her involvement in the iconic soccer tournament, the global superstar released her new song, “Dai Dai” featuring Burna Boy, on May 14.
“Knew from the day you were born/Here in this place, you belong/You’ve been this brave all along/What broke you once made you strong,” she sings to open the anthemic song.
Shakira celebrated the song’s arrival in a post on Instagram. “Dai Dai is here. The @fifaworldcup 2026 starts now!” she cheered.
Credited as the Official FIFA World Cup 2026 song, Shakira filmed a clip announcing “Dai Dai” from Maracanã Stadium in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil.

In a video shared on Instagram last week announcing the song, the Grammy winner appeared in a sporty ensemble, which included a royal blue skort and bright yellow top. The singer was then seen dancing on the soccer field and performing alongside a slew of dancers.
The upbeat tune kicked off with Shakira singing “Oh-eh-oh,” before jumping into the first verse and nailing her choreography.

Known as the Queen of the World Cup, Shakira has been an integral part of the event for 20 years. In 2006, she and Wyclef Jean performed “Hips Don’t Lie” and “Bamboo” at the closing ceremony in Germany.
In 2010, she was tapped to create the official FIFA World Cup song and released “Waka Waka (This Time for Africa).” She performed the hit at both the opening and closing ceremonies in South Africa.
She returned in 2014 to perform “La La La (Brazil 2014)” with Carlinhos Brown at the closing ceremony in Brazil. The song was featured as a track on the official FIFA album.

Soccer fans will be looking forward to seeing the Latin superstar take the stage once again during the first-ever halftime show at the FIFA World Cup final on July 19. She will be joined by fellow headliners Madonna and BTS. The superstars are performing at MetLife Stadium in New Jersey.

The 2026 FIFA World Cup is a celebration across North America, with games taking place in Mexico, the United States and Canada. For the first time in the event’s history, three opening ceremonies will take place in every country.

The first ceremony in Mexico City will kick things off on June 11, followed by ceremonies in Toronto and Los Angeles on June 12. Anitta, Katy Perry, Tyla, Michael Bublé, Alanis Morissette, Alejandro Fernández, J Balvin, Maná and many more stars are expected to headline the opening ceremonies.

title: Dylan Sprouse and Wife Barbara Palvin Expecting First Baby
summary: Dylan Sprouse and Barbara Palvin are going to be parents. The actor and model, who have been married since 2023, announced the baby news on May 14.
story: Dylan Sprouse and Barbara Palvin are going to be parents!
The former “Suite Life of Zack and Cody” star, 33, and his model wife, 32, announced they are expecting their first child together on Instagram May 14.

The couple uploaded a joint post that included a sonogram image of a baby’s hand cutely making a “rock on” symbol. A second image showed Dylan Sprouse and Palvin’s hands jokingly making the same gesture.
TODAY.com has reached out to Dylan Sprouse and Palvin’s reps for a comment.
The couple’s announcement comes the same day Palvin debuted a baby bump when she accompanied Dylan Sprouse to the 79th Annual Cannes Film Festival in Cannes, France.
Palvin posed for photographs while wearing a light blue floor-length dress that accentuated her bump. Dressed in a dark tuxedo, Dylan Sprouse placed his arm around his bride in several pictures.
The two also sweetly cradled her baby bump together in a few snaps.
After five years of dating, Dylan Sprouse and Palvin tied the knot in July 2023 in a ceremony at the model’s parents’ property on the island of Harlekin Birtok in Hungary, Vogue reported at the time.
They exchanged their vows in front of 115 guests in the same Albertirsa church where Palvin’s parents married more than three decades before.
Dylan Sprouse and Palvin got engaged in September 2022.
They publicly confirmed their engagement in a June 2023 interview in V Magazine conducted by Dylan Sprouse’s twin brother, former “Riverdale” star Cole Sprouse.
“We’ve been dating for five years, officially, but we actually met like eight months before at an event,” Palvin shared.
Though sparks flew at that initial meeting, the model later “ghosted” her future groom.
Six months later, she messaged him to suggest the pair meet up in New York.
The “After We Collided” actor responded by saying he had plans to travel to China. Palvin told him she would be in China at the same time to work on a fashion campaign.
When the two finally reconnected, they discovered they shared a sense of humor and a love for “Naruto,” an animated series based on the Japanese manga books of the same name.
“I can tell you the moment I fell in love,” Palvin recalled during the interview. “We started talking about ‘Naruto’ and then he did an impersonation.”
“Oh, yeah. I impersonated Akamaru, he does this spin attack,” Dylan Sprouse remembered.
Added Palvin, “He was like, ‘Akamaru!’ and jumped and turned in the air to land on the bed. And I filmed it in slow motion. It’s my favorite video.”

title: Dan + Shay Announce New Album, ‘Young,’ Out This August
summary: Dan + Shay stopped by TODAY to announce that their sixth studio album, “Young,” will be released in August.
story: Dan + Shay stopped by TODAY with an exciting new announcement for all their fans.
The country music duo, made up of Dan Smyers and Shay Mooney, revealed exclusively on TODAY May 14 that they are releasing a new album titled “Young,” arriving Aug. 21.

“This is our most personal album yet,” Smyers told Carson Daly. “We dug deep. It took us a couple years to work on this. We went left, we went right, we made a bunch of music. And there was some stuff we made, we had cold feet about it. It just didn’t feel like us enough. And as we dug back in — I just feel like it feels authentic.”

“Young” is Dan + Shay’s sixth studio album and centers on family, faith and following their dreams amid this prolific season of their 13-year career, according to their label, Warner Records Nashville.
The album’s title track, “Young,” will be the second single and debuts May 15.

The album’s first single, “Say So,” dropped in April and was what Smyers and Mooney called “the most important release of our career so far.” It’s a powerful track meant to spotlight suicide prevention and dedicated to a late friend and mentor, music executive Ben Vaughn.
Mooney told Carson that the song came about when they went to the writers room and began talking about Vaughn, who died by suicide in January 2025.

“It opened up this can of worms for us, really, to process that grief,” he said. “A lot of times we feel like it comes out of nowhere. We were talking about that — it never really comes out of nowhere. Talking about how we need to be proactive of talking to our friends and making sure they’re OK and making sure we get that out, in therapy or whatever way that is. This song really fell out that day, and it was just a crazy experience to process all these motions.”
Dan + Shay will perform the song May 17 at the ACM Awards, where they are up for duo of the year.
“Young” is the duo’s first album since 2024’s “It’s Officially Christmas: The Double Album” and 2023’s “Bigger Houses,” which included hits like the title track and “Save Me the Trouble.”

Ahead of the release of “Bigger Houses,” Dan + Shay graced Rockefeller Plaza for the Citi Concert Series on TODAY and gave country music lovers the performance of their dreams as they belted out songs like “Tequila” and “Speechless” in the pouring rain.

At the time, Mooney expressed his gratitude for their fans and their support despite the less-than-perfect weather. “Our fans have always been amazing like that,” he said. “I’m blown away every time.”
If you or someone you know is in crisis, call the National Suicide Prevention Lifeline at 800-273-8255, text HOME to 741741 or visit SpeakingOfSuicide.com/resources for additional resources.

title: Valerie Bertinelli Reflects on the ‘Shame’ She Felt in Her Past 2 Relationships
summary: Valerie Bertinelli is opening up about the “shame” she has felt when reflecting on the dysfunctional nature of her last two romantic relationships.“I feel a lot of shame for allowing myself to be treated the way I was treated,” Bertinelli, 66, revealed during an episode of “The Dr.
story: Valerie Bertinelli is opening up about the “shame” she has felt when reflecting on the dysfunctional nature of her last two romantic relationships.
“I feel a lot of shame for allowing myself to be treated the way I was treated,” Bertinelli, 66, revealed during an episode of “The Dr. Ramani Network.”
“A lot of shame,” she added in a clip from the conversation shared on Instagram.

“I can go back to my very first marriage and I understand the painful parts of it, but none of the pain was ever what I experienced in the last two goes at love that I had,” explained the actor and author.

Bertinelli was married to late Van Halen guitarist Eddie Van Halen from 1981 until 2007. They shared one child together, musician Wolfgang Van Halen. She tied the knot with financial planner Tom Vitale in 2011 and divorced him in 2022.
She also dated writer Mike Goodnough for several months in 2024.
Bertinelli told host Dr. Ramani Durvasula, a psychologist and author, that she recognized her former partners’ toxic behavior but “allowed” it to continue.
“I know I’m emotionally intelligent and I know that if I said, ‘Hey, don’t speak to me,’ which I’ve said, ‘Don’t speak to me that way.’ But then I just allow it anyway,” she said.
“So at a certain point, it has to be my responsibility to say ‘I’ve had enough. I’m walking away now,’” she explained.
Dr. Ramani pushed back by telling the former “Hot in Cleveland” star that when a narcissist is called out, their problematic behavior tends to worsen.

“There could be gaslighting. There could be manipulation, There’s being treated like you’re crazy. There could be escalation,” she replied. “At least that’s the experience of most people in unhealthy relationships.”
Dr. Ramani also encouraged Bertinelli to replace the shame she feels with compassion for herself.
Bertinelli told Dr. Ramani she eventually came to the conclusion that she doesn’t want to get involved in another romantic partnership with anyone.
“That’s where my whole responsibility is, to not engage again, which I finally did,” she said.
During a May 7 appearance on TODAY with Jenna & Sheinelle, Bertinelli revealed she hasn’t experienced “intimate love” in more than 10 years.

“I have such exquisite love in my life. I have my animals. I have my girlfriends. I have my son and his beautiful wife. I have my brothers. I have my family. I have beautiful love in my life,” she said.

“But there’s something about intimate love, and I write about it in my book,” she continued. Bertinelli’s part memoir, part self-help guide, titled, “Getting Naked: The Quiet Work of Becoming Perfectly Imperfect,” was published in March.
“There’s something about an intimate love with a partner, and I haven’t had a partner in over a decade at least,” she shared.
Bertinelli added that she felt she hasn’t had a true companion in  a long time.
“I do miss that. But then there’s another part of this, like, I am so independent and I can do everything on my own,” she said.
While speaking to People magazine in March, Bertinelli said she felt she has “never really experienced” true love.
“Two divorces behind me, I felt like a failure. I didn’t know what love actually meant, really, real true (love),” she told the magazine.

“Ed and I had it for glimpses, but I really never really experienced it for any length of time,” she said. “And I still haven’t. You know, Ed was the closest I’ve ever come to feeling true true love.”

title: Dylan Dreyer Just Interviewed Her Cousin on TODAY, and She’s a Famous Influencer
summary: One of Dylan Dreyer’s latest interviews doubled as a mini family reunion when she spoke with influencer Alix Earle, her first cousin once removed.
story: One of Dylan Dreyer’s latest interviews doubled as a mini family reunion.

Alongside Craig Melvin and Al Roker, Dylan chatted with Alix Earle, one of Sports Illustrated Swimsuit’s 2026 cover stars, during the 3rd Hour of TODAY on May 12. At the end of the interview, Dylan mentioned that she and Earle are related to each other.
“We joke that we’re family. I mean, we’re legit family. We’re second cousins,” Dylan said.
The mother of three then turned to Al and said, “It’s kind of like you and Lenny Kravitz,” referring to the TODAY meteorologist and legendary singer being second cousins once removed.
“Only you guys are much better looking, and I’m the odd man out in that trio,” Al joked.
“I feel like the odd man out, too,” Dylan mused.
It turns out, Dylan and Earle’s father, Thomas “TJ” Earle, are first cousins. So his daughter Alix Earle and the TODAY co-host are actually first cousins once removed, rather than second cousins.
“Your dad is one of seven. You have lots of brothers and sisters. You have a million cousins. I mean, the Christmas card when it arrives every year, there’s like 100 people in it,” Dylan said.
Despite their familial ties, Dylan and Alix Earle hadn’t connected until recently.
“Alix and I hadn’t really met until the Kentucky Derby a couple of years ago for the first time,” Dylan said.
Earle and Dylan posed for a photo together at the 2024 Kentucky Derby.

“My cousin’s daughter is my what? Apparently my first cousin once removed. @alix_earle is my first cousin once removed,” Dylan captioned a photo of the duo at the time. “Kind of like Al Roker and Lenny Kravitz. #themoreyouknow So nice to finally meet you Alix…at the 150th @kentuckyderby of all places!”
The following year, Earle and Dylan shared a hug and a chat on the red carpet for the annual horse race.
Earle said on TODAY May 12 that she’s always admired Dylan from afar.
“I grew up watching you all the time, which is why it’s crazy to be sitting here,” she said.
Earle even revealed that she tried her hand at broadcasting while attending college.

“It was like a little club after school. And I was not very good at it, so I quit. But I always wanted to be just like you,” she said. “So this is also so cool getting to sit here and do this with you on such a special day.”

title: Harrison Ford Revisits His Against-All-Odds Origin Story in an Inspiring Commencement Speech
summary: Harrison Ford gave an inspiring commencement speech during Arizona State University’s graduation. Here’s exactly what he said.
story: Harrison Ford wants his own experience to help light the way for the future.

The “Star Wars” and “Indiana Jones” star gave a rousing commencement speech at Arizona State University on May 11, in which he touched on his passion for the environment, how he fell acting and how these graduates have the ability to change the world in ways they may not necessarily realize.

“You made wise choices, followed through with the work. I celebrate your commitment, the combined success of all of you. The potential of your entire generation, that is what gives me hope for the future,” he said.

Ford, who attended Ripon College in Wisconsin, said he didn’t think about the future and “did not make good choices” when he was in college before he signed up for a drama class in the hopes of getting an easy A.
“My classmates were people I had previously discounted as geeks and misfits,” he said. “But I soon realized, I was a geek and a misfit. I had found my fit. These were my people.”
“I began to find myself on stage pretending to be someone else,” he added.

The 83-year-old Oscar-nominated star, who received an honorary Doctor of Arts and Humane Letters in acknowledgement of his worldwide influence, recalled landing more theater work, but not enough where he could make a living as an actor.
“Acting was not yet paying the bills. I was supporting my growing family with carpentry jobs, another way to put food on the table. I loved making things,” he said.
